event: Pre-ceramic Archaic-age peoples migrating through the Lesser Antilles begin seasonal occupation of the island, leaving the earliest evidence of human presence.
Ceramic-age Saladoid Arawak peoples from South America settle the island, establishing agricultural villages and pottery traditions.
Post-Saladoid Arawak communities continue to thrive, with the indigenous name Soualiga ('Land of Salt') reflecting the island's valuable salt ponds.
Christopher Columbus sights the island during his second voyage and names it San Martin after Saint Martin of Tours.
Dutch settlers establish a fort and salt-mining operation at Great Bay, founding the colony that would become Philipsburg's hinterland.
Spain captures the island to deny the Dutch its strategic salt and harbor, fortifying it against rival European powers.
Dutch forces under Peter Stuyvesant fail to retake the island from Spain, with Stuyvesant losing his leg to a cannonball during the siege.
Spain abandons the island, and the Treaty of Concordia partitions it between the Dutch and the French, beginning centuries of shared sovereignty.
The Dutch establish their southern colony of Sint Maarten, separate from the French northern half of Saint-Martin.
Fort Amsterdam and expanding sugar and salt plantations rely increasingly on enslaved Africans brought to the Dutch side.
Philipsburg is founded by Scottish captain John Philips in the service of the Dutch, becoming the capital of the Dutch side.
British forces seize the Dutch side during the Fourth Anglo-Dutch War, one of several wartime occupations of the era.
Britain again occupies the Dutch colony amid the Napoleonic Wars before it is returned to Dutch control.
Following the Napoleonic era, Dutch sovereignty over Sint Maarten is permanently restored under the Treaty of Paris settlements.
Enslaved people on the French northern half are emancipated, prompting many on the Dutch side to flee across the open border for freedom.
The Netherlands abolishes slavery in its Caribbean colonies, emancipating the enslaved population of Sint Maarten on July 1.
The collapse of the salt and plantation economies leaves the island impoverished and heavily dependent on remittances and emigration.
An airstrip is constructed at the future site of Princess Juliana International Airport during the Second World War era.
Princess Juliana International Airport opens, laying the groundwork for the island's future tourism boom.
The Charter for the Kingdom of the Netherlands makes Sint Maarten part of the autonomous Netherlands Antilles within the Kingdom.
The Bobby's Marina and early hotel developments mark the beginning of organized tourism on the Dutch side.
Claude Wathey, a dominant local political figure, accelerates infrastructure and tourism development as a leader of the island.
Rapid hotel, casino, and cruise-port expansion transforms Sint Maarten into a major Caribbean tourism and duty-free shopping destination.
The Treaty of Concordia is reaffirmed and updated through Franco-Dutch agreements maintaining the open border between the two halves.
Hurricane Luis devastates the island, destroying much of its tourism infrastructure and causing widespread damage.
A referendum on the Dutch side shows overwhelming support for separate status as an autonomous country within the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
Preparations advance for the dissolution of the Netherlands Antilles as Sint Maarten finalizes its path to country status.
On October 10, the Netherlands Antilles is dissolved and Sint Maarten becomes an autonomous constituent country within the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
Sint Maarten gains its own parliament, government, and prime minister, with Sarah Wescot-Williams becoming the first prime minister.
Political instability and corruption investigations lead to repeated changes of government and integrity concerns within the young nation.
Category 5 Hurricane Irma strikes Sint Maarten on September 6, causing catastrophic destruction and prompting a major Dutch relief and reconstruction effort.
The Netherlands and Sint Maarten establish a World Bank-managed trust fund to finance the island's post-Irma reconstruction.
The COVID-19 pandemic devastates the tourism-dependent economy, forcing Sint Maarten to accept conditional Dutch financial assistance.
Sint Maarten continues rebuilding its airport and tourism sector while navigating ongoing constitutional and financial relations with the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
